---
id: 'auth-third-party-better-auth'
title: 'Better Auth'
subtitle: 'Use Better Auth as your authentication layer with your Supabase Postgres database'
---

Better Auth can be used as a standalone authentication layer that connects directly to your Supabase Postgres database,
replacing Supabase Auth entirely. This guide focuses on setting up Better Auth on NextJS apps however the general steps
will be the same for any other stack.

If you are using another framework, then see the full [installation docs](https://better-auth.com/docs/installation) for Better Auth. 

## Getting started

1. Install the `better-auth` and `pg` libraries.
2. Set the environment variables: `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET`, `BETTER_AUTH_URL`, and `DATABASE_URL`.
NOTE: Secrets must be 32 characters or more. You can use `openssl rand -base64 32` to generate one.
3. Set up the server-side Better Auth instance.
4. Generate and run the database migration to create the required auth tables.
5. Create a catch-all route handler to expose the Better Auth API.
6. Set up the client-side Better Auth instance to initiate auth operations such as `SignIn` and `SignUp`.

## Install packages

```bash
npm install better-auth pg
npm install --save-dev @types/pg # this may only be required if you run into type errors
```
## Configure environment variables

```bash
BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=<a-random-secret>
BETTER_AUTH_URL=https://<your-app-domain>
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:<password>@db.<supabase-project>.supabase.co:5432/postgres
```

The `DATABASE_URL` should use Supabase's direct connection string (port 5432), not
the transaction pooler (port 6543).

You can find your connection string in `connect` tab at the top of your project dashboard.

## Set up the Better Auth server instance

Create a `lib/auth.ts` file at the root of your project. This file should run server-side only
and should never be imported by client components.

```typescript
import { betterAuth } from 'better-auth'
import { Pool } from 'pg'

export const auth = betterAuth({
  database: new Pool({
    connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
  }),
  emailAndPassword: {
    enabled: true,
  },
  socialProviders: {
    github: {
      clientId: process.env.GITHUB_CLIENT_ID!,
      clientSecret: process.env.GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET!,
    },
  },
  plugins: [admin(), anonymous(), phoneNumber()],
})
```
The above is only an example, there are additional plugins and configuration options to extend authentication
capabilities which can be found [here]( https://better-auth.com/docs/plugins).

## Generate and run the database migration

Run these commands (or their equivalants) from the root of your project.

```bash
npx better-auth generate
```

```bash
npx better-auth migrate
```

The generate command produces an SQL file. The migrate command will run this migration against your database.
Running the migration will create whatever tables are required for your auth configuration.

Create the catch-all route handler to handle all requests to /api/auth/*. The path varies by framework.

```typescript
// app/api/auth/[...all]/route.ts
import { auth } from '@/lib/auth'
import { toNextJsHandler } from 'better-auth/next-js'

export const { GET, POST } = toNextJsHandler(auth)
```

```typescript
// pages/api/auth/[...all].ts
import { auth } from '@/lib/auth'
import { toNextJsHandler } from 'better-auth/next-js'

export default toNextJsHandler(auth)
```

## Set up the Better Auth client instance

Create a `lib/auth-client.ts` file. This can be used client side to call the auth server and perform operations

```typescript
import { createAuthClient } from 'better-auth/react'

export const authClient = createAuthClient({
  baseURL: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL ?? 'http://localhost:3000',
  # only required if auth server is on a different domain than your frontend
})
```

## Usage
```typescript
 import { authClient } from '@/lib/auth-client'

const { data, error } = await authClient.signUp.email({
  email: 'user@example.com',
  password: 'password',
  name: 'John Doe',
})

const { data, error } = await authClient.signIn.email({
  email: 'user@example.com',
  password: 'password',
})
await authClient.signOut()
```

## Limitations

-  Better Auth connects to Postgres as a Superuser, bypassing RLS entirely, hence policies will have no effect. Instead, enforce access control in your server-side API routes by identifying the user from their session and filtering queries accordingly.
